---
title: "API reference"
---

> **What this page is:** Every public symbol in the cositos Python package — functions,
> classes, and their signatures — organized by concern.

The public API splits into these concerns: **serialization**, **embedding**, the
**`Widget` façade**, the **lifecycle reducer**, the **`Transport` seam**, the **protocol
core**, **buffers**, and the **`contrib.controls`** helpers. Most symbols are importable
directly from the top-level package, e.g. `from cositos import dump_document` — **except the
lifecycle-reducer symbols** (`reduce`, `Phase`, `Open`, `Send`, …), which live in the
`cositos.lifecycle` submodule: `from cositos.lifecycle import reduce, Phase, Open`.
Reference checking (`check_references`) is listed under Serialization; capability flags
(`TransportCapabilities`) under the Lifecycle reducer.

**Jump to:**
[Serialization](#serialization) · [Embedding](#embedding) · [Widget](#the-widget-façade) ·
[Lifecycle reducer](#lifecycle) · [Transport](#the-transport-seam) ·
[Protocol core](#protocol-core) · [Buffers](#buffers) · [contrib.controls](#contrib-controls)

The behaviour behind these functions is pinned by the living
[specifications](../extending/specs.qmd) (protocol, serialization, embed, lifecycle,
lifecycle-shell) — the same contracts the test suite and any cross-language port
certify against.

## Serialization

Persist a widget (or a composed graph of widgets) to plain JSON and rebuild it later.
Round-trips are lossless, including binary buffers (base64-encoded per the widget
state-format v2 schema). See the [serialization spec](../extending/specs.qmd#serialization).

| Function | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `dump_model` | `dump_model(entry) -> tuple[str, Record]` | Serialize one `(model_id, state)` entry to a record. |
| `load_model` | `load_model(item) -> tuple[str, dict]` | Inverse of `dump_model`. |
| `dump_document` | `dump_document(entries) -> Document` | Serialize many entries into a v2 Widget-State document envelope. |
| `load_document` | `load_document(doc) -> list[tuple[str, dict]]` | Inverse of `dump_document`. |
| `check_references` | `check_references(doc) -> None` | Validate that every `"IPY_MODEL_<id>"` reference points at a model in `doc`; raises `ValueError` on dangling references. |

```python
from cositos import dump_document, load_document

# a widget's frontend is an ESM string (see the Glossary); any placeholder works here:
ESM = "export default { render({ model, el }) { el.textContent = model.get('n'); } }"
doc = dump_document([("counter", {"_esm": ESM, "n": 3})])
# doc is a JSON-serializable dict; write it, ship it, reload it:
entries = load_document(doc)          # [("counter", {"_esm": ..., "n": 3})]
```

## Embedding

Render a serialized `Document` to self-contained HTML — no kernel required. The anywidget
model/view resolves from a CDN at display time, so no frontend is bundled. See the
[embed spec](../extending/specs.qmd#embed).

| Function | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `embed_snippet` | `embed_snippet(document, *, views=None, requirejs=True, html_manager_version="1") -> str` | Loader + state + view `<script>`s, no page wrapper — drop into an existing page (e.g. a Quarto cell). |
| `embed_html` | `embed_html(document, *, views=None, title="cositos widgets", requirejs=True, html_manager_version="1") -> str` | A complete standalone HTML page. |
| `write_html` | `write_html(path, document, **kwargs) -> None` | Write `embed_html` output to `path`. |

`views` selects which model ids get a rendered view (default: every model). The full
state is always embedded so a referenced model is present for a container to resolve.
A reference (`"IPY_MODEL_<id>"`) only resolves to a child model when the *holding* model
declares a widget-reference trait; plain anywidget (`AnyModel`) widgets do not, so refs
between them stay literal strings (use a `@jupyter-widgets/controls` container to compose
— see `examples/composition/`). Embedded JSON is
script-escaped so a `</script>` inside widget state can't break out of the page.

```python
from cositos import dump_document, write_html

doc = dump_document([("m", {"_esm": ESM, "n": 3})])  # ESM: the widget's frontend module string
write_html("out.html", doc)           # open in any browser (needs internet for the CDN)
```

## The `Widget` façade

`Widget` ties a host state object to a `Transport`. It is deliberately minimal — **no
observer autodetection**: the host decides when state changed and calls `send_state`.

```python
Widget(
    transport,                 # a Transport adapter (see below)
    get_state,                 # () -> dict — current full state (must include "_esm")
    set_state=None,            # (dict) -> None — apply inbound state; omit to ignore updates
    model_id="",               # comm id, for the display mimebundle
    on_custom=None,            # (content, buffers) -> None — handle custom messages
)
```

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `open()` | Send `comm_open`, adopt the transport's real comm id, start listening (if the transport `supports_receive`). Idempotent. |
| `send_state(include=None)` | Send an `update` with the full state, or only the `include` keys. |
| `send_custom(content, buffers=None)` | Send a `custom` message (`model.on("msg:custom")` on the frontend). |
| `close()` | Send `comm_close`. Idempotent. |
| `mimebundle(repr_text="")` | The widget-view mimebundle for display. |
| `_repr_mimebundle_(...)` | Jupyter display hook — rendering the widget opens its comm, then returns the view bundle. |

## Lifecycle reducer {#lifecycle}

`Widget` delegates to a pure **lifecycle reducer** that encodes the widget's open → send
→ close lifecycle as a deterministic state machine. The reducer is a pure function — no
I/O, no side effects, no mutable state.

```python
from cositos.lifecycle import reduce, Phase, Open, TransportCapabilities

state = {"_esm": "export default function render({model, el}) {...}", "value": 0}
phase, effects = reduce(Phase.UNOPENED, Open(), state, TransportCapabilities())
```

### Phases

A widget is always in one of three phases:

| Phase | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `Phase.UNOPENED` | The comm has not been opened yet. |
| `Phase.OPEN` | The comm is open — messages can be sent and received. |
| `Phase.CLOSED` | The comm has been closed. |

### Events

Events are passed to `reduce()` to trigger state transitions. The reducer pattern means
every possible transition is enumerated and fixture-testable.

| Event | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Open()` | User/host wants to open the comm. |
| `SendState(include=None)` | Send an `update` with the full state, or only `include` keys. |
| `SendCustom(content, buffers=[])` | Send a custom message to the frontend. |
| `Inbound(message, buffers=[])` | An inbound message arrived from the transport (buffers already merged). |
| `Close()` | User/host wants to close the comm. |
| `CommIdAssigned(id)` | The transport assigned a comm id after a `comm_open` send. |

### Effects

`reduce()` returns a list of **effects** — commands for the imperative shell to execute.

| Effect | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Send(msg_type, data, buffers, metadata)` | Send a Jupyter comm message to the frontend. |
| `Listen()` | Register an inbound message callback on the transport. |
| `ApplyState(state)` | Apply an inbound state dict to the host object. |
| `InvokeCustom(content, buffers)` | Invoke the host's custom message handler. |
| `Error(message)` | An error — the shell shall raise `RuntimeError`. |

### TransportCapabilities

Capability flags declare which event kinds the transport can carry. The reducer uses
these flags to decide which effects to produce — the shell never blocks or rewrites
events based on capabilities.

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TransportCapabilities:
    supports_receive: bool = True
    supports_request_state: bool = True
    supports_custom: bool = True
    supports_buffers: bool = True
```

- `supports_receive=False` — broadcast-only kernels (early Deno); the reducer suppresses
  the `Listen` effect.
- `supports_buffers=False` — transports that cannot carry binary buffers; `send_custom`
  with buffers produces an `Error` effect.
- `supports_request_state=False` — transports that don't support `request_state`;
  inbound `request_state` messages are silently ignored.
- `supports_custom=False` — transports that don't support custom messages; `send_custom`
  produces an `Error` effect.

See the [lifecycle spec](../extending/specs.qmd#lifecycle) and the
[lifecycle-shell spec](../extending/specs.qmd#lifecycle-shell) for the full contract.

## The `Transport` seam

`Transport` is the **only** kernel-facing interface the core depends on. Each host
(Python `comm`, Deno `Deno.jupyter.broadcast`, IJulia, dotnet-interactive) supplies a
thin adapter; the core contains no I/O. It is a
[`typing.Protocol`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#typing.Protocol) —
implement it structurally, no subclassing required.

```python
@runtime_checkable
class Transport(Protocol):
    supports_receive: bool     # False on broadcast-only kernels (one-way widgets)

    def send(self, msg_type, content, buffers=None, metadata=None) -> None: ...
    def on_message(self, callback) -> None: ...
    # comm_id: str — the real comm id, read defensively and adopted on open()
    #   (not part of the Protocol; supplied by adapters whose comm generates an id)
```

## Protocol core

Pure message shaping and parsing — the language-neutral heart of the library. See the
[protocol spec](../extending/specs.qmd#protocol).

| Symbol | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `PROTOCOL_VERSION` | `str` (`"2.1.0"`) | The widget messaging protocol version cositos targets. |
| `build_comm_open` | `build_comm_open(state) -> tuple[dict, list, dict]` | Build the `comm_open` payload (data, buffers, metadata). |
| `build_update` | `build_update(state) -> tuple[dict, list]` | Build an `update` (`comm_msg`) payload. |
| `build_custom` | `build_custom(content) -> dict` | Build a `custom` message payload. |
| `parse_message` | `parse_message(data)` → `Update` \| `RequestState` \| `Custom` \| `Ignored` | Parse an inbound `comm_msg` into a typed event. |

`parse_message` returns one of four typed events: `Update{state, buffer_paths}`,
`RequestState`, `Custom{content}`, or `Ignored{method}`. An unknown or missing `method`
yields `Ignored` (it never raises), matching ipywidgets' forward-compatible dispatch — the
caller no-ops on it (see the [protocol spec](../extending/specs.qmd#protocol)).

## Buffers

Split binary values out of state and merge them back — a faithful port of the widget
protocol v2 nested-buffer rules.

| Function | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `remove_buffers` | `remove_buffers(state) -> tuple[state, buffer_paths, buffers]` | Extract binary values, replacing each with a hole in the state tree. |
| `put_buffers` | `put_buffers(state, buffer_paths, buffers) -> None` | Inverse; mutates `state` in place. |

## contrib.controls {#contrib-controls}

Helpers that emit **real `@jupyter-widgets/controls` models** (a slider, dropdown, layout
box) as `ModelEntry` state dicts — so you can use stock ipywidgets controls without
reimplementing them and with no `ipywidgets` Python package installed. Import from the
submodule: `from cositos.contrib.controls import int_slider, dropdown, vbox, hbox`.
Each returns a `list[ModelEntry]` (the control plus any companion models it needs, e.g. a
layout/style model), ready to pass to `dump_document`.

| Function | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `int_slider` | `int_slider(value=0, min=0, max=100, **overrides) -> list[ModelEntry]` | An `IntSlider` control. |
| `dropdown` | `dropdown(options, value=None, **overrides) -> list[ModelEntry]` | A `Dropdown` control. |
| `vbox` | `vbox(children, **overrides) -> list[ModelEntry]` | Vertical layout box wrapping child controls. |
| `hbox` | `hbox(children, **overrides) -> list[ModelEntry]` | Horizontal layout box wrapping child controls. |

See the [widget gallery](widgets-gallery.qmd#real-ipywidgets-controls-no-reimplementation-required)
for live examples.
